summary: Incremental view maintenance (IVM) is gaining significant momentum with new implementations from Materialize, Feldera, Epsio, and others, as well as PostgreSQL's experimental pg_ivm extension. Traditional approaches to view updates—either recomputing on every read or periodically refreshing entire datasets—are wasteful and slow. IVM solves this by processing only the deltas, potentially making updates millions of times faster. The post traces the evolution from bag algebra approaches to modern frameworks: timely dataflow provides flexible time modeling, differential dataflow adds version tracking for selective computation reuse, and DBSP offers a simpler circuit-based model that can convert any batch SQL to incremental queries. While these technologies underpin many modern IVM systems, adoption challenges remain due to complexity.
key_points: IVM processes only changed data (deltas) rather than recomputing entire datasets, potentially achieving speedups of 10 million times for large databases with small changes
Traditional bag algebra approaches work for simple queries but struggle with complex, recursive, or nested structures
Timely dataflow's key innovation is separating data and control planes, allowing concurrent processing at different points in time
Differential dataflow tracks multiple versions of data states in a partial order, enabling selective reuse of prior computations
DBSP uses digital signal processing concepts to prove that any batch SQL can be automatically converted to incremental queries using just four operators
money_quotes: In practice this may be an improvement of several orders of magnitude. For our example above |DB|≅10⁹ and |ΔDB|≅10², this can make QΔ 10 million times faster!
Cheap updates allow us to refresh materialized views more frequently. In doing so, we can keep materialized views more up to date with their source data, thereby reducing data latency.
Complex incremental queries that use joins, window functions, or recursive algorithms are very difficult to write by hand. A more systematic approach is needed.
I like to think of timely dataflow a lot like Hadoop's Map/Reduce: a powerful, but low-level framework for data processing.
DBSP can convert any batch-based SQL to an incremental query.

The progression from timely dataflow to differential dataflow and then to DBSP shows a clear trajectory: moving from highly flexible, low-level frameworks toward more structured, to easier-to-use incremental computation systems.
Differential dataflow is just really complicated.
Getting this technology into everyone's hands will be a very big deal.
key_insight: Modern IVM engines built on timely dataflow, differential dataflow, and DBSP can automatically convert any batch SQL query into an efficient incremental computation, but the technology's complexity remains a barrier to widespread adoption.
